topic: Religion & Ethics: Normative Ethical Theories (Natural Law, Situation Ethics, Kantian, Utilitarianism)
subject: religious-studies

items.q: What does natural law theory base morality on?
items.correct: Human nature and reason — there are objective purposes (telos) built into humans which reason can discover, generating universal moral precepts.
items.misconception: Natural law means whatever happens in nature is morally right.
items.random_wrong: Natural law is the same thing as the laws of physics.
items.why: The 'naturalistic fallacy' confusion — students assume natural law endorses lions eating gazelles or natural disasters. Aquinas's theory concerns human nature and rational ends, not all natural events.

items.q: What does utilitarianism say is right action?
items.correct: The action that produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number — judged by consequences, not intentions or rules.
items.misconception: Whatever makes you personally happy is the right thing to do.
items.random_wrong: Utilitarianism says you should always tell the truth.
items.why: Students reduce utilitarianism to egoism. It is impartial — your own happiness counts no more than anyone else's. Personal happiness alone misses the aggregative core.

items.q: What is the central principle of situation ethics?
items.correct: The only intrinsically good thing is agape love; rules can be broken if doing so produces the most loving outcome in the situation.
items.misconception: You should do whatever feels right in the moment.
items.random_wrong: Situation ethics says rules should always be followed.
items.why: Situation ethics is principled (love), not just intuition or relativism. Students collapse it into 'whatever feels right' — Fletcher's actual theory is more disciplined than that.

items.q: Is Kant's categorical imperative the same as the Golden Rule?
items.correct: No — Kant explicitly rejected the Golden Rule as a basis: it depends on personal preferences (a criminal might waive punishment). The categorical imperative tests whether maxims can be universal laws.
items.misconception: Kant's categorical imperative is just the Golden Rule: treat others as you'd like to be treated.
items.random_wrong: Kant's categorical imperative says we should follow our feelings.
items.why: Kant footnotes the difference in the Groundwork: the Golden Rule cannot ground duties to oneself and bends to inclination. Universalisability is a logical, not preferential, test.

items.q: Did Bentham distinguish higher and lower pleasures?
items.correct: No — that is Mill's qualitative refinement; for Bentham all pleasures count alike and differ only quantitatively ('pushpin is as good as poetry'), measured by the hedonic calculus.
items.misconception: Bentham's hedonic calculus ranks intellectual pleasures as higher in quality than bodily ones.
items.random_wrong: Bentham thought pleasure was morally irrelevant.
items.why: Attributing higher/lower pleasures to Bentham reverses the Bentham-Mill development that questions love to test. Bentham is the pure quantitative hedonist; Mill adds quality.

items.q: How many factors in Bentham's hedonic calculus?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 7. “10” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Singer. “Mill” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Exact answer: 1863 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
